data.title: Eniro Group Names Interim CFO
data.description:
Eniro Group said Friday it appointed Stefan Liljedahl as its interim chief financial officer, effective March 10.

The Nordic technology company added that it will continue the recruitment process for a permanent successor to Joel Odland, who decided to step down as CFO by the end of the first quarter, as announced previously.

data.title: Cocoa Futures Approach 3-Month Lows
data.description:
Cocoa futures dropped below $9,600 per tonne, nearing a three-month low, as demand concerns mounted following record-high prices.

Hershey Co. and Mondelez International Inc. indicated that further price hikes may be on the way, as chocolate makers grapple with sustained high cocoa costs.

Meanwhile, cocoa farmers in the Ivory Coast reported recently that the mid-crop harvest prospects look poor due to the long dry season.

Cocoa hit an all-time high above $12,600 per tonne in December mainly due to crop problems and adverse weather in Ivory Coast and Ghana, which produce about 60% of the world's cocoa.

Adding to supply concerns, a dispute in Ivory Coast between major exporters and local shippers has created uncertainty.

Meanwhile, ICE-monitored cocoa stocks in US ports, which had been falling for 1.5 years, have recently recovered to 1,403,965 bags after hitting a record low of about 90,000 tons.

data.title: TSX Brief: Down Another Near 60 Pts, Set For Third Successive Losing Day Amid Lower Oil and Gold Prices
US Stocks Swing, UnitedHealth Drags Dow Lower
data.description:
US stocks fluctuated on Friday, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq hovering near the flatline, while the Dow Jones dropped around 280 points.

The health and energy sectors were among the biggest laggards.

Shares of UnitedHealth plunged almost 10% after The Wall Street Journal reported that the Justice Department has launched an investigation into the company’s Medicare billing practices in recent months, citing sources familiar with the matter.

On the other hand, communication services outperformed.

Also, AI-related stocks traded higher, including Nvidia (0.6%), boosted by strong earnings from Alibaba.

Meanwhile, traders are awaiting flash S&P Global PMIs for insights into private sector performance this month.

For the week, the S&P 500 remains little changed, while the Nasdaq has lost 0.3%, and the Dow is down 0.8% so far.

data.title: Oil's Stable Period Could Last Longer, Commerzbank Says — Market Talk
data.description: Oil's low volatility in recent weeks could last for some time, according to Commerzbank Research. "The implied volatility for Brent is as low as it was last seen in the summer of 2024," commodity analyst Carsten Fritsch says. "Despite all the conflicts, there have been no significant supply shortfalls, sanctions have been circumvented and demand is rising less at the same time." Oil prices are currently on track for weekly gains of roughly 1% amid fears of supply disruptions in Kazakhstan and uncertainty around a potential peace deal in Ukraine. However, according to Fritsch, oil's stability is also supported by OPEC+'s ability to address any potential supply shortages thanks to its ample spare capacity after years of production cuts. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: Nasdaq Composite Rises 35.62 Points (0.18%)
Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0932 ET - Oil's low volatility in recent weeks could last for some time, according to Commerzbank Research. "The implied volatility for Brent is as low as it was last seen in the summer of 2024," commodity analyst Carsten Fritsch says. "Despite all the conflicts, there have been no significant supply shortfalls, sanctions have been circumvented and demand is rising less at the same time." Oil prices are currently on track for weekly gains of roughly 1% amid fears of supply disruptions in Kazakhstan and uncertainty around a potential peace deal in Ukraine. However, according to Fritsch, oil's stability is also supported by OPEC+'s ability to address any potential supply shortages thanks to its ample spare capacity after years of production cuts. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0910 ET - U.S. natural gas picks up from yesterday's retreat with a widening inventory deficit keeping futures well above $4/mmBtu across the curve. This week's Arctic weather is expected to be reflected in a seventh straight triple-digit storage draw in next week's EIA report, and winter conditions are seen extending into March. "After the warm spell, some predict it will get cold again. This expectation is maintaining the demand for natural gas," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. The Nymex front month is up 2.9% at $4.273/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0849 ET - Five-year U.S. inflation breakevens finished Thursday at 2.66%, the highest end-of-day level since March 2023, according to U.S. Treasury data. That's still well below the levels of around 3.5% that 5-year breakevens tested during 2022, but represents a significant rise in inflation jitters over the past 6 months. "Inflation concerns are once again percolating," research firm Strategas writes, noting that the increase has come despite range-bound oil prices. The Fed minutes this week highlighted potential inflation effects from tariffs: "Business contacts in a number of Districts had indicated that firms would attempt to pass on to consumers higher input costs arising from potential tariffs," the central bank said in its record of its January meeting. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

0702 ET - Fincantieri could report solid results for 2024 and its fourth quarter on March 24, BNP Paribas analyst Giuseppe Grimaldi says. The Italian shipbuilder is expected to report double-digit growth in sales in the fourth quarter and a 39% increase in Ebitda, he says, noting that all of the group's business lines are performing better. That includes cruises, which had weakened during the Covid-19 pandemic. Fincantieri's shares have risen over 20% year-to-date, almost catching up with other EU defense peers, Grimaldi says. BNP Paribas reiterates its neutral rating but increases its target price on the stock to 10 euros from 6.1 euros. This is Fincantieri's third target price upgrade in a week, following those by Intesa Sanpaolo on Feb. 19 and Equita on Feb. 14. Shares are up 2% at 8.9 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0639 ET - The improving purchasing managers' index no longer indicates a collapse in manufacturing production, Deutsche Bank's chief economist for Germany, Robin Winkler, says in a note. The manufacturing PMI rose to a nine-month high of 47.3 in February, albeit still indicating contraction. However, the downside is that the data show industrial companies still expect a sharp decline in employment, consistent with continued reports of job cuts at various companies, he says. Moreover, although the order situation stabilized at the start of the year, it remains to be seen whether this is not just the calm before the storm, Winkler says. "Export-orientated companies are currently benefiting from a weak euro, anticipating a potential tariff conflict with the U.S.," he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0626 ET - Brent crude has been hovering around $75 a barrel since the beginning of the month likely due to pending policy decisions that could shift price direction, according to Commerzbank Research. "Oil prices appear to have been 'frozen' [...] a number of decisions are pending that could drive the oil price in one direction or the other," says Barbara Lambrecht. Future price movements will depend on OPEC+'s output decision, negotiations to end the war in Ukraine, the U.S.'s stance on Iran and President Trump's plan to rebuild strategic reserves, the commodity analyst says. According to Commerzbank, OPEC+ will likely delay its planned oil output increase to ensure the market doesn't risk a significant supply surplus and prevent prices from declining in the course of the year. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0516 ET - Palm oil ended higher, supported by an improving regional economic landscape and the potential for short-covering activities ahead of the weekend, which could provide a supportive tone to the market, according to Kenanga Futures analysts in a commentary. Looking ahead, the next key resistance is at MYR4,700/ton, according to Phillip Nova analyst Lim Tai An. A successful breakout above this level could propel prices to MYR4,770/ton, while failure to break through would likely lead to a retracement towards the MYR4,550 level, the analyst says. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for May delivery rose MYR22 to MYR4,664/ton. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0511 ET - OPEC+ is expected to delay the output hike planned for early April amid an uncertain economic and geopolitical outlook, according to analysts at ANZ Research. "The downside for prices is high if OPEC proceeds with production hikes," they say. The cartel and its allies face potential challenges from shifting U.S. policy and risks of a trade war, while renewed maximum pressure on Iran, sanctions on Russia and the halt of Venezuelan crude oil purchases add to the complexity. At the same time, rising fiscal pressure pushes OPEC members to focus on maximizing revenue, according to ANZ. "OPEC's ability to phase out production cuts without disrupting the oil market is getting increasingly difficult," the analysts say. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0407 ET - Crude futures edge lower in early trade but are still poised for weekly gains of more than 1.5% on rising supply concerns and a weaker U.S. dollar. Prices are supported by disruptions to Kazakhstan's oil flows after a Ukrainian drone attack on a major pipeline in Russia and prospects that OPEC+ could delay its output hike plans. The market is also keeping a close eye on developments in U.S.-Russia talks to end the war in Ukraine. Meanwhile, the latest data showed U.S. crude stockpiles rose more than expected last week, while gasoline inventories and distillate fuel stocks fell. According to analysts at Saxo, "the market continues to lack a clear direction, with supply disruptions in Kazakhstan and the OPEC+ production increase delay being offset by global demand worries." Brent crude is down 0.7% to $75.93 a barrel, while WTI falls 0.8% to $71.92 a barrel. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0331 ET - Sembcorp Industries is poised to post earnings CAGR of over 10% through 2028, as its three key segments enter expansion mode, DBS Group Research's Pei Hwa Ho says in a research report. Its renewables segment may deliver profit CAGR of over 20%, supported by its project pipeline which is set to increase attributable installed capacity by around 50% over next 2-3 years, the analyst says. Its gas-related services segment's earnings could increase, partly thanks to Singapore's healthy power market, the analyst says. Its integrated urban solutions segment's profit may alsoo rise, driven by potentially rapid growth in demand for industrial parks in Asean. DBS raises the stock's target price to S$7.38 from S$7.35 and maintains a buy rating. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0112 ET - Samsung Electro-Mechanics is set to benefit this year from demand for its high-end chip package substrates for AI devices, Heungkuk Securities analyst Heechul Park writes in a note. Demand for the company's high-performance flip-chip ball grid array substrates should rise as the supply of cost-efficient AI servers increases, Park says. Its FC-BGA substrates for AI accelerators will start generating revenue from 2025, he adds. He expects operating profit at the company to jump 27% in 2025, following 14% growth in 2024. Heungkuk raises its target price for the stock by 12% to KRW190,000 and keeps a buy rating. Shares are 0.5% lower at KRW143,200. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: DJIA Falls 230.66 Points (0.52%)
DA Davidson Adjusts Builders FirstSource Price Target to $157 From $175
data.description: Builders FirstSource has an average rating of overweight and mean price target of $184.35, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: Eyes on Ukraine as intense talks continue. Russia could help fund reconstruction
data.description:
There are a couple of notable reports on Ukraine today and both point to a near-term conclusion of the war.

The first is from Reuters, which reports that Russia could agree to using part of the $300 billion of sovereign assets frozen in Europe for reconstruction in Ukraine but will insist that part of the money is spent on the one-fifth of the country that's controlled by Russia.

A separate report from Axios said US and Ukrainian officials negotiated "all night" into today to conclude a minerals deal in what US officials called a "make or break scenario fro the Ukrainians". The terms of the deal are not clear.

data.title: SolGold, Former CEO Agree Settlement Deal
data.description:
SolGold said Friday it resolved its former chief executive officer's unfair dismissal claim against the company.

While terms of the settlement deal are confidential, the miner said it made a payment to Darryl Cuzzubbo.

data.title: UnitedHealth Shares Tumble 10% Over Medicare Billing Inquiry
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group (UNH, Financial) shares fell sharply this morning after authorities opened an investigation into its Medicare Advantage billing practices. The U.S. Department of Justice launched a probe to examine whether UNH records diagnoses in ways that secure extra payments from government programs. Investors reacted immediately as pre-market trading showed a decline of more than 10%, with shares dropping over $52 to trade below $447.

Market participants are discussing the impact of the inquiry on the nation's largest Medicare Advantage provider. Regulators find that if they find any irregularities, all that could change the billing practices and affect not just UNH but the broader healthcare industry. The risks associated with aggressive billing methods were being looked at by traders and analysts, and others were cautiously hopeful that the company would deal with whatever problems as quickly as possible.

The case highlights the significance and need for compliance and transparency in the healthcare arena. The Justice Department has not yet given investors further details on the scope or lasting consequences of the statements. The discussions on trading platforms continue as the situation conveys the market's sensitivity to regulatory action and the fact that even industry leaders must follow strict standards.

data.title: Sterling Likely to Rise Gradually Versus the Euro — Market Talk
data.description: Sterling is likely to continue rising gradually against the euro as U.K. growth outperforms the eurozone, Monex Europe analyst Nick Rees says in a note. While U.K. growth is weak, it's still marginally better compared to the eurozone, he says. The U.K.'s less challenging policy environment and more limited political headwinds are also factors that could lead to sterling outperformance. "Indeed, this has been our base case for some time, and we see little evidence from today's [U.K. and eurozone purchasing manager' surveys] that would suggest a change is warranted." The U.K. composite purchasing managers' index fell to 50.5 in February from 50.6 in January while the eurozone composite PMI held steady at 50.2. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: RingCentral Is Maintained at Neutral by Piper Sandler
data.description: (14:35 GMT) RingCentral Price Target Cut to $31.00/Share From $38.00 by Piper Sandler

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0935 ET - Sterling is likely to continue rising gradually against the euro as U.K. growth outperforms the eurozone, Monex Europe analyst Nick Rees says in a note. While U.K. growth is weak, it's still marginally better compared to the eurozone, he says. The U.K.'s less challenging policy environment and more limited political headwinds are also factors that could lead to sterling outperformance. "Indeed, this has been our base case for some time, and we see little evidence from today's [U.K. and eurozone purchasing manager' surveys] that would suggest a change is warranted." The U.K. composite purchasing managers' index fell to 50.5 in February from 50.6 in January while the eurozone composite PMI held steady at 50.2. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0908 ET - With retail sales in Canada faltering in January after the previous month's jump, the needle doesn't move for the Bank of Canada's rate decision next month, Capital Economics' Stephen Brown says. He expects the BoC's call will hinge on the 4Q GDP release next week and whether U.S. tariffs scheduled for March 4 come into effect or not. Still, the economist notes the upside surprise in retail sales volumes in December, which suggests monthly GDP rose 0.3% on-month rather than the 0.2% he previously anticipated. That offers a better hand-off to the new year, though likely only pushed 4Q GDP growth to 1.9% annualized from the 1.8% previously expected, he says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0851 ET - Worries about a potential glut of U.S. government debt are easing, encouraging investors to buy Treasurys. As a result, yields are weakening. The Fed has indicated it could pause its program to reduce bond holdings, in which case the supply available for investors would be contained. Meanwhile, the Treasury said it isn't planning to increase long-term bond issuance. January home sales data is due at 10 a.m. ET. The 10-year yield is at 4.494% and the two-year at 4.273%, both around yesterday's levels and on path to end this shortened week with small gains. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0849 ET - Five-year U.S. inflation breakevens finished Thursday at 2.66%, the highest end-of-day level since March 2023, according to U.S. Treasury data. That's still well below the levels of around 3.5% that 5-year breakevens tested during 2022, but represents a significant rise in inflation jitters over the past 6 months. "Inflation concerns are once again percolating," research firm Strategas writes, noting that the increase has come despite range-bound oil prices. The Fed minutes this week highlighted potential inflation effects from tariffs: "Business contacts in a number of Districts had indicated that firms would attempt to pass on to consumers higher input costs arising from potential tariffs," the central bank said in its record of its January meeting. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

0837 ET - A surge in Canadian retail sales at the end of last year is another indication the economy perked up in the final quarter of 2024. Sales jumped 2.5% on-month in December, the strongest advance since May 2022. That left sales for the quarter up 2.4%, accelerating from growth of 1.1% the quarter before. Gains were broad, with sales at motor vehicle and parts dealers up 1.9% and core sales ahead 2.5% for the month thanks to a lift in food and beverage trade. That suggests the federal tax holiday that began mid-December likely played only a small part in the strength. The caveat is shoppers look to have pared back in the new year, with early indications pointing to a 0.4% fall in January sales. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0825 ET - Spain's economy would face a limited adverse impact from the effect of U.S. tariffs on goods imports, according to the governor of its central bank. A 10% import tariff imposed by the Trump administration on European Union goods exports would hit Spanish GDP by 0.11 percentage points, including retaliatory tariffs, or 0.03 points without them, Jose Luis Escriva said in a speech in Barcelona, quoting Bank of Spain forecasts. That compares with a higher impact on the eurozone of 0.24 points, or 0.16 points without EU reprisals, he said. Meanwhile, tariffs plus retaliatory measures would increase Spanish inflation by 0.07 percentage points more than otherwise forecast. But the impact on the U.S. would be starker, he said, with the bank predicting a 0.99 percentage-point hit to GDP and 0.63 point rise in inflation. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0821 ET - The euro could benefit if the German government formed after Sunday's federal election delivers more expansionary fiscal policy, Commerzbank's currency analyst Ulrich Leuchtmann says in a note. Germany currently appears to be the "sick man of Europe" with weak growth that is dragging down the eurozone economy, he says. "Many market participants may suspect that a more expansionary fiscal policy could reduce the problem." This could have a moderately positive effect on the euro. While this would push government debt higher, it would only marginally change the assessment of the euro-area's fiscal situation. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0819 ET - The fall in the French composite PMI to a 17-month low, while the rest of the eurozone performed better, suggests that the budget agreed at the start of February has dealt a significant blow to business sentiment, Berenberg's Andrew Wishart says in a note. Indeed, excluding France, the composite PMI would have increased to 51.5 in February, the highest since June 2024, rather than stagnating at 50.2, he says. That offers confidence that the European Central Bank interest-rate cuts and rising real incomes will support a return to modest growth, Wishart says. Still, France will likely remain a problem amid a deeply divided parliament, and a workable coalition in Germany post-Sunday's election is critical to maintaining the recent improvement in German economic activity, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0723 ET - Eurozone business sentiment paused its upward trend of recent months, according to February's purchasing managers' index,Commerzbank's Vincent Stamer writes in a note. The stagnation of the PMIs--the composite index was 50.2, the same as January--also conceals an enormous discrepancy between France and Germany, he says. Political uncertainty and proposed austerity measures are weighing on sentiment in France's economy, Stamer says. Moreover, as sentiment indicators are still at a low level, they point to only a moderate upturn in the euro area economy. Any positive effect of lower European Central Bank interest rates is only likely to unfold in the second half of the year, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0710 ET - Germany's sovereign credit default swaps, which protect against the risk of the country defaulting, stay steady ahead of German elections this weekend. The elections pose risks for investors, says Karsten Junius at J. Safra Sarasin Sustainable Asset Management. Friedrich Merz of the Christian Democratic Union is likely to become the next chancellor. However, it is unclear whether traditional parties will secure at least two thirds of the seats in parliament. This would raise concerns over whether they could change the constitution and reform the debt brake rule without the support of populist parties, the asset manager's chief economist says. Germany's five-year CDS stays unchanged at 13 basis points, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0709 ET - The euro is vulnerable as markets aren't pricing in much risk of a stronger-than-expected result from the far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) party in Germany's federal election Sunday, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. "Polls currently place the center-right CDU/CSU alliance in the lead at 30% followed by AfD at 20% and the Social Democratic Party at 15%," he says. While markets aren't expecting a better result for the AfD, markets could still find good value in selling the euro ahead of the election given the single currency's recent gains, Pesole says. ING remains negative on the euro against the dollar. The euro falls 0.3% to $1.0474. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0656 ET - The main concern from February's eurozone purchasing managers' surveys come from inflationary signals, with both input and output prices showing signs of acceleration, says Christophe Boucher, chief investment officer at ABN AMRO Investment Solutions. This raises the risk that the European Central Bank might not cut rates as much as expected in 2025, he says in a note. Comments this week given by policymaker Isabel Schnabel advocating for a pause in ECB rate cuts this week align with this concern. However, in his view upside risks to inflation are fading, and the ECB needs to normalize its monetary policy quickly to support the rebound in the manufacturing sector. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

data.title: Croesus appoints Jean Lavigueur to its Board of Directors
data.description:
Croesus appoints Jean Lavigueur to its Board of Directors

Canada NewsWire

MONTRÉAL, Feb. 21, 2025

MONTRÉAL, Feb. 21, 2025 /CNW/ - Croesus announced today the appointment of Jean Lavigueur as a new member of its Board of Directors. Mr. Lavigueur will join the current team and contribute his extensive experience in business and high technology to the company.

Before joining the Board of Directors at Croesus, Jean Lavigueur served as the Chief Financial Officer of Coveo Solutions for seventeen years, where he currently remains a senior advisor. Prior to his time at Coveo, he co-founded Taleo (NASD: TLEO) and acted as its Chief Financial Officer for seven years. Lavigueur was also the Chief Financial Officer at Baan SCS (NASD: BAANF) and Groupe Berclain, each for seven years. Additionally, he spent seven years as an auditor and tax specialist at Coopers & Lybrand.

Throughout his career, Lavigueur has been involved with several boards of directors, including those of Cossette Communications (TSX: KOS), Opsens (TSX: OPS), Vention, and Petal Health. With a passion for business, Jean Lavigueur leverages his expertise and vision to support companies with ambitious growth plans.

"We are honoured to welcome Jean Lavigueur to our Board of Directors," said Rémy Therrien, founder and executive chairman. "His addition aligns perfectly with Croesus's growth strategy, which is focused on developing new products to provide our clients with increasingly effective solutions."

"I am thrilled to join the Board of Directors at Croesus and contribute to the growth of this leading FinTech company," said Lavigueur. "I am excited to work with such an experienced and passionate team, and I look forward to collaborating with each member," he added.

"We are delighted to welcome Jean to our Board of Directors," said Vincent Fraser, President and CEO of Croesus. "His extensive experience in business management and high technology will enhance our already experienced team. Croesus aims to solidify its status as a leader in wealth management technologies, and Jean's addition will greatly contribute to achieving this vision."

About Croesus

Croesus provides innovative, high-performance, and secure wealth management solutions that include portfolio management systems, portfolio rebalancing tools, and application programming interfaces (APIs). These solutions empower wealth management professionals to improve their productivity, enhance their client relationships, make informed decisions, and maximize the management of their assets under management. Croesus's mission is to provide a superior experience to its clients, users, partners, and employees and to positively impact the community. With more than 200 employees in its Montréal, Toronto, and Geneva offices, Croesus has won several industry awards for being a high-quality solution provider and an outstanding employer.

SOURCE Croesus

View original content to download multimedia: http://www.newswire.ca/en/releases/archive/February2025/21/c2359.html

/CONTACT:

Frédéric Le Bouar, Director of Product Marketing and Business Promotion, Croesus, communications@croesus.com, 1-855-243-6101

Copyright CNW Group 2025

data.title: LCI INDUSTRIES SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
LCI Industries, a leading supplier of components for the recreational vehicle (RV) and adjacent industries, has released its 2024 Form 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in a competitive market environment.

Financial Highlights

  • Net sales: $3,741.2 million, a decrease of 1% from 2023 primarily due to decreased industry production levels in certain markets, offset by a 7% increase in North American RV wholesale shipments.
  • Gross profit: $879.7 million, reflecting improved operating efficiencies and cost reductions.
  • Operating profit: $218.2 million, an increase from $123.4 million in 2023, driven by decreases in material, freight, and warranty costs.
  • Net income: $142.9 million, a significant increase from $64.2 million in 2023, attributed to improved operating profit margins.
  • Diluted EPS: $5.60, compared to $2.52 in 2023, reflecting the increase in net income.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The company operates in two primary segments: OEM and Aftermarket. The OEM Segment contributed 76% of consolidated net sales, focusing on RVs and adjacent industries like boats, buses, and manufactured homes. The Aftermarket Segment, which includes discretionary accessories and replacement parts, contributed 24% of net sales.
  • Geographical Performance: The company's operations are global, with manufacturing and distribution facilities across North America and Europe. International sales represented approximately 11% of consolidated net sales in 2024, consistent with 2023.
  • Sales Units: In 2024, sales to RV OEMs were $1.7 billion, representing 61% of OEM Segment net sales. The company sold 1,061,000 hitches in 2024, up from 910,000 in 2023, through its subsidiary CURT Manufacturing LLC.
  • New Product Launches: The company continues to innovate with a diverse portfolio, including chassis and suspension solutions, outdoor living systems, and interior and appliance solutions.
  • New Production Launches: The company invested in automation and efficiency, consolidating certain facilities in 2023 and 2024 to reduce overhead and enhance margin stability. Capital expenditures included $6 million in capacity investments for operational improvements.
  • Future Outlook: The company aims to maintain market leadership in the North American RV market while expanding in aftermarkets and adjacent industries. It plans to leverage innovation and operational excellence to gain additional market share.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Expansion: The company has strategically expanded its Aftermarket Segment through acquisitions, including Curt Manufacturing LLC and Kaspar Ranch Hand Equipment, LLC, to strengthen its product portfolio and market reach. Additionally, the company has diversified its portfolio beyond the RV OEM market into marine, building products, transportation, international, and aftermarket sectors to mitigate seasonal and cyclical market risks.
  • Capital Management: The company maintained a level of liquidity sufficient to meet short-term cash needs and managed its cash and capital structure to maximize shareholder return. It repaid $89.2 million of indebtedness in 2024, reducing interest expense. The company also paid quarterly dividends aggregating $4.30 per share, totaling $109.5 million. No shares were repurchased during 2024 and 2023 under the stock repurchase program authorized in May 2022 for up to $200 million over three years.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue managing its capital requirements, working capital needs, and debt levels to support future strategic investments. It estimates 2025 capital expenditures of $50 to $70 million, focusing on automation and lean projects. The company expects to maintain compliance with financial covenants and anticipates that its operating cash flows and credit facilities will be sufficient to fund future payments and long-term initiatives. The effective income tax rate for 2025 is estimated to be approximately 24 to 26 percent.

Challenges and Risks

  • Competitive Industry: The company operates in highly competitive industries with low barriers to entry, which could lead to increased competition and pressure on pricing and market share. The company's reliance on a few key customers, with two customers accounting for 34% of consolidated net sales in 2024, poses a risk if these relationships are disrupted.
  • Economic and Business Factors: Economic and business factors beyond the company's control, such as cyclicality and seasonality in the RV and other industries, could lead to fluctuations in operating results. The availability of wholesale and retail financing is crucial, and any reduction could limit consumer purchases and dealer inventories, impacting demand for the company's products.
  • Raw Material Costs: Volatile raw material costs, particularly for steel and aluminum, could adversely affect financial condition and operating results. The company faces risks from geopolitical conditions, such as tariffs and trade tensions, which could disrupt supply chains.
  • Management and Cybersecurity: The loss of key management could impair the company's ability to execute its business strategy. Cybersecurity threats pose a risk to operations and reputation.
  • Regulatory Compliance: Regulatory compliance, including environmental and data privacy laws, could increase costs and liabilities.
  • Market Diversification: The company is focusing on diversification beyond the RV market into marine, building products, transportation, international, and aftermarket sectors to mitigate seasonal and cyclical market risks. Strategic acquisitions are part of the company's growth strategy, but integration challenges and unforeseen liabilities could impact financial performance.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations, which may adversely affect its financial results. Interest rate increases could raise borrowing costs, impacting financial condition and cash flows. The company’s debt agreements contain covenants that may restrict operations and acquisitions, and failure to comply could have a material adverse impact.

SEC Filing: LCI INDUSTRIES [ LCII ] - 10-K - Feb. 21, 2025

data.title: Hims & Hers Health Shares Fall Pre-Bell as US FDA Says Ozempic, Wegovy Shortages Have Ended
Hims & Hers Health Acquires California Peptide Facility
data.description:
Hims & Hers Health said Friday that it has acquired a peptide facility based in California.

The company said the acquisition will strengthen the durability of its US supply chain.

Financial terms were not disclosed.

Hims & Hers Health shares were down 17% in premarket trading.

data.title: Freshpet Is Maintained at Overweight by Piper Sandler
data.description: (14:29 GMT) Freshpet Price Target Cut to $160.00/Share From $180.00 by Piper Sandler
